behaviors.description: Products marked as out-of-stock are excluded from the recommendation results regardless of their relevance score
behaviors.justification: The filtering logic must be tested against actual product inventory data integrated with the recommendation flow. A unit test of a filter function alone would miss bugs in how catalog/inventory lookups are queried or passed to the filtering logic. Mocking the catalog would hide real integration failures.
behaviors.test_description: Load a product catalog into a test database with 50% in-stock and 50% out-of-stock products having similar relevance scores. Generate recommendations for a user. Verify that the returned list contains zero out-of-stock items, even if some out-of-stock products ranked highly by the algorithm.

behaviors.description: The user preference filter correctly excludes product categories the user has explicitly blocked in their settings
behaviors.justification: Preference filtering must integrate user settings retrieval with the category exclusion logic. Unit testing a filter function in isolation would not catch bugs in how blocked categories are loaded or applied across the full recommendation pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with 3 categories marked as blocked in their preference settings. Generate recommendations with a catalog containing products from those blocked categories that would normally rank highly. Verify no products from blocked categories appear in results.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm ranks products that match the user's recent browsing patterns higher than products with no browsing signal
behaviors.justification: The ranking algorithm must correctly ingest real user browsing history and apply it as a weighting factor. Mocking browsing data would hide bugs in data retrieval, aggregation, or how the algorithm applies browsing signals relative to other factors.
behaviors.test_description: Create two user profiles: User A with 20 electronics browsing events in the past week, User B with no browsing history. Generate recommendations for both. Verify User A's results rank electronics products higher, while User B's results show more diverse/popularity-based products.

behaviors.description: Pagination returns the correct page of results, with stable ordering — requesting page 2 twice returns identical results if the underlying data hasn't changed
behaviors.justification: Ordering stability depends on how the underlying data store returns results. In-memory mocks might appear stable, but real database query ordering issues would be hidden. Database sort order, index behavior, and result set consistency must be tested against actual storage.
behaviors.test_description: Store 500 products with consistent ranking scores. Request page 2 (items 21-40) twice with no intervening changes. Verify both responses return identical product IDs in identical order. Repeat with page 3 to verify consistency across pagination boundaries.

behaviors.description: The recommendation algorithm surfaces at least one product from a category the user hasn't browsed before (serendipity requirement), avoiding a filter bubble
behaviors.justification: Serendipity logic must be tested with the full algorithm and realistic product catalog. Unit testing the serendipity function alone would not catch failures in how unbrowsed categories are identified or how the logic is integrated into overall ranking. If serendipity uses randomness, this requires real non-deterministic testing (Agentic risk).
behaviors.test_description: Create a user with browsing history in only 2 product categories (electronics, clothing). Generate recommendations with a catalog spanning 6 categories (electronics, clothing, home, sports, books, toys). Verify that at least one product from categories not in the user's browsing history appears in the top 10 results.

behaviors.description: A new user with no purchase or browsing history receives recommendations based on overall popularity rather than an empty result set
behaviors.justification: Cold-start behavior must be tested against actual popularity metrics and product catalog. A unit test of the popularity fallback would not catch failures in how new users are detected or how popularity data is loaded and applied.
behaviors.test_description: Create a new user with zero purchase and browsing history. Generate recommendations. Verify the response is non-empty (contains at least 10 products) and that returned products are sorted by a popularity metric (sales, ratings, trending score).

behaviors.description: Click tracking records the correct product ID, position in the list, and timestamp for each recommendation the user interacts with
behaviors.justification: Click tracking must write to actual event storage (database, event log, queue). Mocking event storage would hide bugs in data serialization, timestamp accuracy, or persistence. The full end-to-end flow from click to storage must be tested.
behaviors.test_description: Generate recommendations (10 products). Simulate a click on position 3. Verify the click event is recorded in the tracking store with: correct product_id (matching position 3), correct position (3), and timestamp within 1 second of click. Repeat with clicks on positions 1, 5, and 10.

behaviors.description: The response time for generating recommendations stays under 200ms for a catalog of 100,000 products
behaviors.justification: Performance requirements must be tested against realistic infrastructure. In-memory unit or integration tests hide real bottlenecks: database query latency, network round-trips, serialization overhead. Real (or realistically simulated) hardware and I/O patterns are required to measure actual latency.
behaviors.test_description: Load 100,000 products with realistic attributes (title, category, price, ratings, popularity score) into a production-like database. Warm up caches and JIT compilation with 5 calls. Measure latency for 50 recommendation requests across diverse user profiles. Verify 95th percentile latency stays under 200ms and p99 under 300ms.

behaviors.description: When a recommended product is purchased by the user, the recommendation algorithm deprioritizes it in subsequent requests
behaviors.justification: Deprioritization must be tested with the full system: purchase tracking must flow through to the ranking algorithm, and the algorithm must apply deprioritization correctly. Unit testing the deprioritization logic alone would not catch bugs in purchase data retrieval or integration into the ranking step.
behaviors.test_description: Generate recommendations for a user (note the top-ranked product). Simulate purchase of that product. Generate recommendations again for the same user 1 minute later. Verify the previously purchased product either does not appear in the new results or ranks significantly lower (not in top 5).

behaviors.description: The recommendation results are deterministic for the same user state — requesting recommendations twice with no intervening user activity returns the same ranked list
behaviors.justification: Determinism depends on the full system, not just the algorithm. Non-determinism can arise from database query ordering, floating-point arithmetic, hidden state, or non-deterministic data fetching. Testing at unit level (algorithm function only) would miss these integration-level bugs.
behaviors.test_description: Set up a user profile with specific purchase, browsing, and preference history. Generate recommendations at time T (call 1). Verify no intervening events occur. Generate recommendations again at time T+1 second (call 2). Verify both calls return identical product IDs in identical order. Repeat 5 times with different user profiles.
